summary:
The Harmandir Sahib — Golden Temple (c. 1588–1604 AD, with later additions) in Amritsar is the holiest shrine of [[event:3506|Sikhism]] — a spectacular white marble and gold-covered temple built in the middle of a sacred pool (Sarovar) by the fifth Sikh Guru, Arjan Dev Ji, that embodies Sikh principles of equality and openness through its physical design. Unlike Hindu temples or Muslim mosques, the Harmandir Sahib was deliberately built with four entrances (one on each side), symbolizing its openness to people of all four directions and all backgrounds regardless of caste or religion — a physical expression of Guru Nanak's teachings on equality. Guru Arjan Dev Ji installed the Adi Granth (the Sikh scripture) in the temple upon its completion in 1604, establishing it as both the spiritual center and the administrative headquarters of the growing Sikh community.

The temple's history has been turbulent. The [[event:2507|Mughal Emperor Aurangzeb]] damaged it during the Mughal-Sikh conflict, and the Afghan ruler Ahmad Shah Durrani desecrated and partially destroyed it in 1762 — but Sikh forces restored it each time. In the 19th century, [[event:2369|Maharaja Ranjit Singh]] covered its upper floors with gold leaf, giving it the common name 'Golden Temple.' The temple's Langar (free community kitchen) serves approximately 100,000 free meals daily regardless of the visitor's religion, background, or means — the world's largest community kitchen and a living demonstration of Sikh values. The 1984 storming of the temple by Indian Army troops (Operation Blue Star) to remove armed Sikh militants led to Prime Minister Indira Gandhi's assassination by her Sikh bodyguards and remains a defining trauma in modern Sikh-Indian relations.
